coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and their use for selective determination of Pb2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Idrees, Muhammad
Saqib, Najmus
Zaman, Gohar
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots were synthesized from fenugreek seeds through a single step hydrothermal method. The method is simple, fast, pleasant to the environment and cheaper. The CDs were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), UV–visible spectrophotometer, X-ray diffraction (XRD), High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and fluorescence. The CDs obtained were extremely fluorescent. The fluorescent carbon dots exhibited excitation-dependent behavior with the maximum excitation at 372 nm. The interaction of CDs was studied with different selected cations Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cu+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Sn4+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Sr2+, (NH4)6Mo7O24, 4H2O, Cr6+, Sb3+, Ba2+, Li+, and Mg2+. None of the ions studied showed any effect on its fluorescence intensity except Pb2+ which decreased its intensity. A direct relationship was found between Pb2+ concentrations and quenching of CDs intensity. Detection limit (DL) and quantification limits (QL) were determined as three and ten times of the standard deviation of the blank for ten number of measurements. DL and QL were found in the order 9.345 μM and 31.15 μM respectively. This linear behavior between quenching and Pb2+ concentration is useful for analytical purpose.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescent Pb2+ Sensor
Fenugreek
Hydrothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of pH, NaCl, Storage Time and optimum temperature on Fenugreek-CDs
Interaction of different selected cations to Fenugreek-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are grateful to HEC-Pakistan for financial support of the work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical characteristics of the prepared CDs (A) UV–vis absorption inset: photographs of CDs solution under day and UV light (365 nm). (B) Excitation, and emission spectra (λemiss = 458 nm) of Fenugreek-CDs in aqueous solution (C) Emission spectra of Fenugreek-CDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelength (λexc) (D) CIE chromaticity coordinates of Fenugreek-CDs.
(A) XRD pattern, (B) FT-IR spectrum, (C) TEM image and (D) grain size of Fenugreek-CDs.
Effect of different parameters on Fenugreek-CDs. (A) pH (B) Effect of KCl (C) Storage Time (D) Optimum Temperature for Synthesis.
(A) Quenching of Fenugreek-CDs fluorescence by different metal ions (25 ppm) and (B) Quenching of Fenugreek-CDs fluorescence by different metal ions (25 ppm) and Pb2+ (25 ppm) in 1:1 (λem = 458 nm).
(A) Fluorescence quenching of Fenugreek-CDs by stepwise addition of Pb2+. (B) Stern-Volmer plot for quenching of Fenugreek-CDs by Pb2+.
QY and synthesis method of CDs prepared from different natural materials.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and their use for selective determination of Pb2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry Bacha Khan University, Charsadda, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa 24420, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry Bacha Khan University, Charsadda, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa 24420, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots were synthesized from fenugreek seeds through a single step hydrothermal method. The method is simple, fast, pleasant to the environment and cheaper. The CDs were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), UV–visible spectrophotometer, X-ray diffraction (XRD), High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and fluorescence. The CDs obtained were extremely fluorescent. The fluorescent carbon dots exhibited excitation-dependent behavior with the maximum excitation at 372 nm. The interaction of CDs was studied with different selected cations Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cu+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Sn4+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Sr2+, (NH4)6Mo7O24, 4H2O, Cr6+, Sb3+, Ba2+, Li+, and Mg2+. None of the ions studied showed any effect on its fluorescence intensity except Pb2+ which decreased its intensity. A direct relationship was found between Pb2+ concentrations and quenching of CDs intensity. Detection limit (DL) and quantification limits (QL) were determined as three and ten times of the standard deviation of the blank for ten number of measurements. DL and QL were found in the order 9.345 μM and 31.15 μM respectively. This linear behavior between quenching and Pb2+ concentration is useful for analytical purpose.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of C-dots both by top-down and bottom-up approaches from different precursors and by different methods remained the area of interest for many investigators across the world. The search for different methods and different precursors is to find a cheap, easy and affordable method and precursor for better sensitivity and selectivity. C-dots have been synthesized from plants, fruits, polymers etc. The great attention to synthesis of C-dots from plants and fruits is due to their easy availability, low prices, and environment friendly [1]. C-dots have applications in bio imaging, delivery of drugs and more importantly they have been explored as a sensor for determination of inorganic ions (Hg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr6+, Be2+, Co2+, Al3+) and organic pollutants [2].

C-dots possesses high biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, high fluorescent properties and high potential as a sensor for biomolecules and ions (cations and anions) [3]. Therefore many researchers remained interested in the search of new precursors and new methods for C-dots synthesis in order to use them for metals determination in water and biological samples instead of using expensive, complicated methods. Also trained persons are required to run the instruments like atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [4–6].

Lead is one of the most popular harmful heavy metal. Its bioaccumulation occur in the cardiovascular and nervous system of human on their exposure to a Pb2+ contaminated water and air. In a higher concentration it can cause anemia, dysfunction of reproductive and nervous system. It may cause developmental disorders, etc. In addition, exorbitant level of Pb2+ can even cause the death of an individual [7]. Therefore, in this project we developed a sensitive, selective and fast method for the determination of Pb2+ at trace level.

The Fenugreek seed was purchased from local supermarket (Charsadda, Pakistan). Quinine sulphate, standard buffer solutions, AlCl3, CaCl2, CdCl2, CrCl3, CoCl2, CuCl2, CuCl, FeCl2, FeCl3, KCl, SnCl4, NaCl, NiCl2, PbCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, Sr(NO3)2, (NH4)6Mo7O24, 4H2O, Cr2O7, SbCl3, BaCl2, LiCl, and MgCl2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All reagents were utilized of analytical grade with no further purification.. The nitrate and chloride salts of metal ions were used for the preparation of metal ions solution. The double de-ionized water was utilized throughout the work.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by a UV-1800 spectrophotometer 240 v (Shimadzu Company, made in japan, Serial no. All 455010154). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on an LS-45 spectrofluorometer (Perkin Elmer, Waltham, Massachusetts, United States).The samples morphologies were determined by a JEM- 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. FTIR measurements were performed on a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). XRD was performed on JDX-3532 X- ray diffractometer with Cu Kα (Wavelength = 1.5418 Å) and 2Theta-Range (0 to 160°) at 20–40 kV and 2.5–30 mA. (JEOL, Japan). XPS analysis were performed on K-Alpha Thermo Scientific, USA.

CDs were prepared from fenugreek seeds (carbon precursor) by a hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 1.5 g ground Fenugreek seed was added to 30 mL distilled water and agitated for 1 h. The mixture was heated in an oven for 8 h in an autoclave lined with Teflon at 180 ◦C. The suspension was then centrifuged (16000 rpm, ½ h) and filtered through a 0.22 μm micron filter. The washing was performed for 36 h by using deionized water though a dialysis membrane (500–1000 MWCO). The CDs solution which was brown in colour was finally freeze-dried and reserved.

Lead (Pb2+) in different concentrations (3.95 × 10−5–3.95 × 10−4 mol/L) were mixed with 0.5 mL (15 μg/mL) solution of Fenugreek-CDs and diluted to 2.5 mL with deionized water. The mixture was incubated for 2 min at room temperature and then the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was recorded. The experiments were performed in replicates.

The UV–Vis spectrum of Fenugreek-CDs (Fig. 1 A), showed peaks at 224 nm and at about 283 nm. The peaks at 224 and 283 nm are due to π → π⁎ transitions of the CC bond (of aromatic sp2 domains from the carbon core of the CDs), and n → π⁎ transitions of the CO bond, respectively [8,9,10]. The Fenugreek-CDs solution showed a brown colour (left) under daylight while a bright blue fluorescence was observed under UV light at 365 nm wavelength (right) (Fig. 1A inset).The synthesized carbon dots have excitation max at 372 nm and emission were at 458 nm Fig. 1B. Excitation wavelength from 332 nm to 482 nm with an interval of 10 nm between the two measurements was scanned for maximum emission of Fenugreek-CDs (Fig. 1C). The high emission intensity was found at 458 nm with an excitation wavelength of 372 nm. The quantum yield of Fenugreek-CDs was measured to be about 27.1 %. CIE chromaticity coordinates of Fenugreek-CDs is shown in Fig. 1D.

The CDs quantum yield was determined by using a method already established. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 0.54 at 340 nm) was used as a standard. In order to minimize the reabsorption effects, absorbencies in the 1 cm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength (360 nm) [9]. The QY of the CDs was calculated by the following equation:
Q =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
(
n R 2
)
Where Q show the quantum yield, I fluorescent intensity, n represent the solvent refractive index, and A is the absorbance. The subscript R refers to the corresponding parameter of known fluorescent standard. Quantum yield of CDs prepared from different precursors are listed in Table 1.

XRD provides the cluster nature of the surface of the synthesized CDs. The broad peak obtained in XRD indicates the high degree of disorderness of carbon atoms on the surface of the CDs. A broad diffraction peak was observed around 23.1° (Fig. 2 A), which was consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of carbon based materials [17]. FTIR results (Fig. 2B) showed a very broad band of absorption at around 3339 cm−1. The band is due to stretching vibrations of OH. Another peak at 1636 cm−1 is because of a stretching vibration between C and O (CO), The peak at 1267 cm−1 is because of NH stretching of aromatic amines and beak at 1012 cm−1 stretching NH [13]. Fenugreek seeds (CDs) were found spherical and monodisperse. The particle size were about 10 nm (Fig. 2C).

The constituent and surface states of the Fenugreek-CDs were characterized by XPS (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3A shows that the obtained Fenugreek-CDs predominantly contained carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. Computed from the XPS spectrum, the C: O: N atomic ratio in the Fenugreek-CDs was 57.29: 32.58: 10.13. In comprehensive, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) showed four peaks at 283.9, 284.38, 285.83 and 288.18 eV, which were attributed to CC/CC, C-N, C-O and C = O/C = N, respectively [14]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) showed three peaks at 399.4 400.05 and 400.38 eV, which were ascribed to C-N-C, C-N and N–H, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) presented two peaks at 531.59 and 532.72 eV, which were ascribed to CO and C-O-C/C-OH, individually [15,16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of pH, NaCl, Storage Time and optimum temperature on Fenugreek-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH study on the intensity of Fenugreek seeds derived CDs showed (Fig. 4 A), that the intensity remained stable when the pH was not less than 5. Therefore, all study was performed in distilled water without pH adjustment of the solution (pH 7.0). Moreover, Fenugreek-CDs displayed high stability in high KCl solution (Fig. 4B). Fenugreek-CDs showed excellent stability and the intensity remained stable for 40 days when they are preserved in acetone at-4 °C. Temperature optimization for maximum synthesis of CDs (Fig. 4D) showed that the 180 °C was the optimum temperature for the synthesis of Fenugreek-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of different selected cations to Fenugreek-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Interaction of different selected cations such as Ni2+, Sr2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, K+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Na+, Zn2+, Cu+, Mn2+, Mo6+, Sb3+, Ba2+, Li+, Mg2+ and Cr6+ with Fenugreek-CDs was studied to examine the consequences of the studied cations on the fluorescence behavior of Fenugreek-CDs Fig. 5 A and B. All the studied cations neither quenched nor enhanced the intensity of C-dots fluorescence except Pb2+, which quenched the intensity of Fenugreek-CDs fluorescence. Selectivity of Fenugreek-CDs for Pb2+ is very important and was checked by using equal concentration of the other cations collectively with Pb2+. It was found in the results that only Pb2+ quenched the intensity of Fenugreek-CDs while other cations did not show any effect on its intensity, proving its selectivity for Pb2+.

A decrease in the quenching of Fenugreek-CDs intensity is shown in Fig. 6 A & B. The gradual addition of Pb2+ to a solution of Fenugreek-CDs causes its gradual decrease in its intensity, while the shape and position of the fluorescence emission peak did not change (Fig. 6 A). Moreover a linear relation was found between the quenching of Fenugreek-CDs intensity and the concentration of Pb2+. This linear relation between the quenching and Pb2+ is useful for analytical purpose.
(1)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K s
v
[ P
b
( 2 + )
]
Io is the Fenugreek-CDs intensity in the absence of Pb2+, I is the intensity of Fenugreek-CDs in the presence of Pb2+ and
K
sv
is the Stern-Volmer constant. The relationship between the decrease in the intensity of Fenugreek-CDs fluorescence and Pb2+ were explained by the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1) that produce the Stern- Volmer constant (5.856 × 103 M). Stern-Volmer constant gives the sensitivity of Fenugreek-CDs for the detection of Pb2+. Higher the Stern-Volmer constant greater the sensitivity will be and vice versa. Detection limit (DL) and quantification limits (QL) were determined as three and ten times of the standard deviation of the blank for ten number of measurements. DL and QL were found in the order 9.345μ M and 31.15 μM respectively.

The suitable concentration of Fenugreek-CDs was determined. The optimum concentration of CDs for their maximum quenching was determined by mixing both the quencher and CDs solution in equal concentration. Quenching was found maximum with 15 μg/mL CDs concentration. Therefore, 15 μg/mL of Fenugreek-CDs was used in following experiments. In order to find out the selectivity of Fenugreek-CDs for the determination of metal ions a total of 21 ions (Ni2+, Sr2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, K+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Na+, Zn2+, Cu+, Mn2+, Mo6+, Sb3+, Ba2+, Li+, Mg2+ and Cr6+) were used in the study. All the studied ions did not show any effect on the intensity of CDs fluorescence except Pb2+ which quenched CDs fluorescence. The quenching is due to a coordination relation among the OH− groups present on the surface of fenugreek seeds and Pb2+. Another factor that contribute to the quenching is due to the non-radioactive electron transfer between the vacant d-orbitals of Pb2+ and amine groups present on fenugreek seeds derived CDs surface [18,19].

The quantum yield from different precursors and different methods were compared (Table 1 ). The results obtained in the current project shows that quantum yield in the current work is higher from some of the reported works in the literature.

A green and environment friendly hydrothermal method was used for carbon dots synthesis from Fenugreek seeds. The synthesized CDs were found useful for the sensitive and selective detection of Pb2+ ions in aqueous solution. The quenching of CDs fluorescence by Pb2+ is attributed to a coordination complexes between OH– and Pb2+. The quenching could also be attributed to the non-radioactive electron transfer between the amine groups on CDs surface and Pb2+.Fenugreek-CDs gives high quantum yield and better limit of detection. The method is simple, selective, sensitive and less expensive for the detection of Pb2+ ions in natural water samples. It is thus our belief that CDs prepared from various natural resources are environmental friendly and are worthy to be used for the detection of different metal ions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to HEC-Pakistan for financial support of the work.
